feature: Pusher Channels — Real-Time Sync for Shopping Lists

overview.purpose: Replace one-time fetch with real-time sync so shared list members see changes instantly.
overview.architecture: Client subscribes to private Pusher channel per list. Server triggers events on list mutations. Client updates local state on received events.
overview.pattern: Server-authoritative: all writes go through Vercel API → API writes to Neon DB → API triggers Pusher event → all subscribed clients receive update.

client.note: Lightweight client library. No React wrapper needed — use a context provider with native pusher-js.

server.note: Node.js server SDK for triggering events and authorizing channels. Install at project root (not in /api/).

pricing.recommendation: Sandbox is sufficient for early users. A household of 2-4 people = 2-4 concurrent connections. Shopping list mutations are low-frequency (tens per day, not thousands).

steps: Select cluster closest to users (eu for Switzerland: eu)
Note: App ID, Key, Secret, Cluster from App Keys tab

variables.name: PUSHER_KEY
variables.description: Pusher app key — safe to expose to client (public)

variables.name: PUSHER_SECRET
variables.description: Pusher app secret — server only, never expose to client

setup.vercel: Add via Vercel dashboard or `vercel env add`. PUSHER_APP_ID and PUSHER_SECRET as 'Sensitive'. PUSHER_KEY and PUSHER_CLUSTER as plain.
setup.local: Add to .env.local (already in .gitignore): VITE_PUSHER_KEY, VITE_PUSHER_CLUSTER for client. PUSHER_APP_ID, PUSHER_KEY, PUSHER_SECRET, PUSHER_CLUSTER for API.

naming.rationale: One channel per shopping list. Private prefix requires authorization — only list members can subscribe.

whyPrivateNotPresence.reason: Presence channels are limited to 100 members and add overhead tracking who's online. We only need event broadcasting, not online awareness. Avatar row shows members from DB, not live presence.
whyPrivateNotPresence.future: If 'who's currently shopping' indicator is ever needed, upgrade to presence-list-{listId}.

channelStrategy.whyNotPublic: Public channels have no authorization. Anyone could subscribe to any list's events if they guessed the channel name.

sharedInstance.description: Shared Pusher server instance reused across all API routes.

code.export: Named export: getPusher() with lazy initialization

authEndpoint.description: Authorizes client to subscribe to private channels. Verifies JWT, checks list membership, returns signed auth token.
flow: 1. Extract Bearer token from Authorization header (same pattern as shopping-list-items.js)
2. Verify JWT via jose + Neon Auth JWKS
3. Extract socket_id and channel_name from request body
5. Query DB: is this user the list owner OR an accepted share member?

requestBody.socket_id: string — provided by pusher-js client automatically

triggerEvents.description: Add Pusher triggers to existing API routes. Each mutation endpoint triggers an event after successful DB write.
triggerEvents.excludeSender: Pass socket_id from request to exclude the sender from receiving their own event. Client sends X-Pusher-Socket-Id header.

pusherContext.description: React context that initializes pusher-js once, provides subscribe/unsubscribe helpers, and handles reconnection.
responsibilities: Initialize Pusher client with VITE_PUSHER_KEY and VITE_PUSHER_CLUSTER
Configure channelAuthorization to point to /api/pusher-auth with Bearer token
Expose pusher instance and connection state

channelAuthorization.headersProvider: async () => { const token = await getAuthToken(); return { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` }; }
channelAuthorization.note: headersProvider is async — pusher-js supports this. Reuses getAuthToken from shoppingListApi.js

connectionStates.reconnection: pusher-js handles automatic reconnection with exponential backoff. No custom retry logic needed.
connectionStates.offlineBehavior: When connection drops, client continues working with local state. On reconnect, do a one-time full fetch to reconcile.

contextNesting.description: PusherContext wraps inside AuthContext but outside AppContext.
order: PusherContext — needs auth token for channel authorization
AppContext — uses Pusher to subscribe to list channels

subscriptionLifecycle.where: AppContext.jsx (or a custom hook used by AppContext)
flow: 1. When shoppingList loads (or active list changes), subscribe to private-list-{listId}
2. Bind event handlers: item:added, item:updated, item:removed, list:cleared, list:renamed, member:*
3. Each handler updates local React state (shoppingList / sharedListItems)
4. When switching lists: unsubscribe old channel, subscribe new channel
5. On logout: unsubscribe all channels, disconnect Pusher

eventHandlers.item:added: Append item to local list state (if not already present by entryId — dedup)
eventHandlers.item:updated: Find item by entryId, update checked state
eventHandlers.item:removed: Filter item out of local list state by entryId
eventHandlers.list:cleared: Set list state to empty array
eventHandlers.list:renamed: Update list name in local state
eventHandlers.member:joined: Add member to local members list, update avatar row
eventHandlers.member:left: Remove member from local members list
eventHandlers.member:removed: If current user: redirect to fresh list + show toast. If other: remove from members list.

excludeSender.description: When the local user adds/checks/deletes an item, they already see the optimistic update. They should NOT receive their own Pusher event (would cause flicker or duplicate).
excludeSender.mechanism: Client sends its socket_id in API requests via X-Pusher-Socket-Id header. Server passes this to pusher.trigger() to exclude that connection.
implementation: shoppingListApi.js includes X-Pusher-Socket-Id header on all mutation requests
Server-side: pusher.trigger(channel, event, data, { socket_id: req.headers['x-pusher-socket-id'] })

reconciliation.description: Handle the gap between disconnect and reconnect. Events fired while offline are lost.
reconciliation.strategy: On pusher connection state change to 'connected' (after being disconnected), do a full fetch of the current list from the API. This reconciles any missed events.
reconciliation.implementation: Bind to pusher.connection state_change. If previousState was 'unavailable' or 'connecting' and newState is 'connected', trigger fetchShoppingList().

userAddsItem: 2. Optimistic update: item appears in local state immediately
6. All OTHER subscribed clients receive 'item:added' event
7. Their event handler appends item to local state
8. Sender does NOT receive event (excluded via socket_id)
userChecksItem: 6. Other clients receive event, update checkbox state
userDeletesItem: 2. Optimistic update: item removed from local state
6. Other clients receive event, remove item from state

securityModel.channelAuth: Private channels require server-side authorization. Auth endpoint verifies JWT + checks DB for list membership before returning signed token.
securityModel.noSecretOnClient: PUSHER_SECRET never leaves the server. Client only has PUSHER_KEY (public) and PUSHER_CLUSTER.
securityModel.jwtVerification: Reuses existing Neon Auth JWKS verification pattern from other API routes.
securityModel.channelIsolation: Each list has its own channel. Users can only subscribe to channels for lists they're a member of.

implementationOrder.details: Create Pusher app, add env vars to Vercel and .env.local
Create api/_lib/pusher.js with lazy-initialized Pusher server SDK
Create api/pusher-auth.js — JWT verification + list membership check + channel authorization

implementationOrder.task: Server: add triggers to existing endpoints
implementationOrder.details: Modify api/shopping-list-items.js to trigger Pusher events after DB writes. Read X-Pusher-Socket-Id header for sender exclusion.
Create src/context/PusherContext.jsx — initialize pusher-js, auth config, expose instance + socketId
Subscribe to list channel, bind event handlers to update local state, handle reconnection reconciliation
Update shoppingListApi.js to include X-Pusher-Socket-Id header on POST/PATCH/DELETE
Open app in two browsers/tabs with different users on a shared list. Verify: add/check/delete syncs in real time. Verify: sender doesn't get duplicate. Verify: reconnection reconciles.

edgeCases.pusherDown: App continues working normally — just without real-time sync. All mutations still go through API → DB. Users see updates on next manual refresh or page reload.
edgeCases.tokenExpiry: headersProvider fetches a fresh token on each auth request. If session expires, Pusher subscription will fail with 403 — same as any other API call.
edgeCases.duplicateEvents: Dedup by entryId in event handlers. If item already exists in state (by entryId), skip the add. Prevents double-add on race conditions.
edgeCases.eventOrdering: Pusher delivers events in order per channel. No reordering concern within a single list.
edgeCases.tabVisibility: pusher-js automatically manages connection when tab is backgrounded. No custom visibility handling needed.
edgeCases.multipleListsSubscribed: Only subscribe to the currently active list's channel. Unsubscribe when switching. Saves connections and messages on the free tier.
edgeCases.largeLists: Pusher message size limit: 10KB. A single item event is ~200 bytes. Even list:cleared with 100 items is fine since we just send {} and client clears local state.
edgeCases.rateLimits: Pusher free tier: 200K messages/day. At 4 users doing 50 mutations/day = 200 events × 3 recipients = 600 messages/day. Well within limits.

futureConsiderations.presenceChannels: If 'who is currently shopping' indicator is needed, upgrade channel prefix to presence-list-{listId}. Adds member_added/member_removed lifecycle events with user info.
futureConsiderations.encryptedChannels: If list contents are sensitive, use private-encrypted-list-{listId} prefix with encryptionMasterKeyBase64. Pusher cannot read event data.
futureConsiderations.webhooks: Pusher supports server-side webhooks for channel_occupied/channel_vacated events. Useful for analytics (when was list last active?).
futureConsiderations.batchTriggers: If multiple items are added at once (e.g., meal plan → shopping list), use pusher.triggerBatch() to send all events in one API call.
